schools.summary: 'Ben-hadad' is the inherited Syrian royal title; this is Benhadad II, son of a king victorious over Omri, and the thirty-two kings are tributary chieftains.
Matthew Henry: Israel's apostasy, not Syria's strength, is the real cause; sin puts a people out of God's protection.
Gill identifies this as Benhadad the second, son of the king Asa once hired against Baasha.
The demand and Ahab's answer are the formal assertion and acceptance of Syrian suzerainty; Ahab's submissiveness only emboldens Benhadad.
Gill: Ahab stays shut in the city, not daring to meet the invader; Benhadad claims all as good as in his hand.
The itemized demand (treasure, then wives and children) is the deliberate escalation of a suzerain asserting total ownership over his vassal.
Gill: Benhadad calls it his 'not of right' but by force, Ahab being unable to resist.
Henry: a disgraceful submission springing from sin — guilt dispirits men and makes them cowards, so Ahab will buy his life on any terms.
Clarke: Ahab hoped his humiliation might soften the tyrant and win better terms.
Ellicott: Ahab surrenders, but not at discretion; he accepts vassalage yet not the sack of the city.
Gill: Ahab acknowledges Benhadad's sovereignty over him.
K&D: Ahab's submissiveness only makes Benhadad more audacious, prompting a second, heavier embassy.
Henry: Benhadad's pride rises upon the submission; the more Ahab humbles himself, the higher his enemy mounts.
Gill notes only that the messengers returned from Benhadad, the return itself signalling contempt for the surrender.
Ellicott: the demand is virtually for the plunder of Samaria and probably neither expects nor desires acceptance — a refusal of all but unconditional surrender.
Clarke: Benhadad means to sack the whole city; Jarchi's guess that the prize was the book of the law is noted but set aside.
Henry: covetous, spiteful, and unreasonable, Benhadad orders the seizing of what Ahab most loves and the plunder even of his subjects' houses.

debates.question: How does the second demand differ from the first?

sides.position: it is a demand to sack and plunder the whole city, not merely take tribute
the special object was the book of the law of the Lord (Jarchi's conjecture, noted and doubted)

schools.summary: Ellicott: the 'elders of the land' are the northern heirs of the ancient Mosaic senate of elders, a body of advice and concurrence, though largely overborne by royal power.
Gill: roused by the last message, Ahab wisely calls a council of the elders.
Clarke: with everything at stake, the elders choose desperate defence over degrading and ruinous surrender.
Henry: the elders speak bravely and promise to stand by Ahab, their courage supplying what the king lacked.
Gill: elders and people are unanimous in refusing.
K&D: Ahab grants the first demand but refuses the second — tribute yes, but not the ransacking of the city.
Henry: a modest denial that still owns Benhadad's dominion, and one Ahab durst not have made without his people's backing.
Gill: the refusal is real but delivered in a poor, sneaking manner, resting on the elders' encouragement.
Ellicott: 'the dust of Samaria' threatens the city's demolition and boasts overwhelming numbers; Josephus and a drunken, feasting Benhadad are cited.
Clarke: the Targum reads the boast as of an army so vast the ground cannot hold them.
Henry: an oath of impotent rage; the very extravagance of his pride prepares Benhadad for ruin.
Gill: the threat is spoken to Ahab.

debates.question: What does the boast about 'the dust of Samaria' mean?

sides.position: a threat to raze the city to dust, joined to a boast of vast numbers
a boast that his army is so numerous the dust will not suffice for each soldier's feet (Targum)

schools.summary: K&D: the proverb equals the Latin 'sing no triumph before the victory'; the armed man may not boast as one disarmed after the fight.
Clarke: a proverbial saying, confirmed by the Targum of Jonathan.
Henry: one of the wisest words Ahab ever spoke; apply it to spiritual conflict — Peter fell by confidence; be never off your watch.
Gill: Ahab answered mildly and wisely.
The 'pavilions' are branch booths (succoth); the order 'simu' is a soldier's command to array for the storming of the city, not to build a stockade (against the LXX).
Henry: Benhadad's drunken security is the forerunner of his fall, like Belshazzar's; woe to the land whose king is such a child.
Gill: drinking at noon shows Benhadad and the kings were addicted to intemperance.

The prophet's appearance shows Jezebel's persecution had abated and the schools of the prophets were reviving; Ahab receives the word with reverence, while Elijah reserves himself for a higher mission.
Henry: God sends mercy to wicked Ahab for Israel's sake, to magnify his mercy, and to mortify Benhadad's pride.
Gill relays the tradition that the prophet was Micaiah son of Imlah.
Clarke wonders that neither Elijah nor Elisha appears, and asks whether this was one of them in disguise.

sides.position: Micaiah the son of Imlah (Jewish tradition)
possibly Elijah or Elisha in disguise

themes: 'that thou shalt know that I am the LORD'

schools.summary: The victory is assigned to the 'young men' (retainers or armour-bearers of the provincial chiefs), and Ahab himself must 'begin the fray' — the marginal reading of the second question.
Gill: Ahab's 'By whom?' is the doubt of a king with no army fit to face the Syrians.
232 picked retainers and a garrison of 7000 fit only to man the walls; the sally is at noon while the Syrians rest. K&D deny the 7000 are those of 1 Kings 19:18.
Clarke: the 232 are Ahab's bodyguard, which he must lead; and (per Jarchi) the 7000 may be those who had not bowed to Baal.
Henry: God chooses weak and foolish things to confound the strong, that the power may appear to be his.

debates.question: Are the 7000 the same 7000 who had not bowed to Baal (1 Kings 19:18)?

sides.position: yes, or at least the same in number
no — the sameness of number is coincidental

schools.summary: K&D: the sally is at noon, the very hour Benhadad and the thirty-two kings are drinking themselves drunk in the booths.
Gill: the young men go out boldly, but the text seems to show Ahab did not lead them out at their head.
Gill: the young men march out of Samaria first, before the 7000 who follow.
K&D: informed that men have come out, Benhadad in drunken arrogance orders them taken alive.
K&D: the drunken order to take the attackers alive exposes the Syrians to slaughter, for captors cannot fight like killers.
Clarke: confident of victory, Benhadad wants them brought in alive for useful information.
Henry: the order is both imprudent and unjust; drink makes him forget the policies and justice of war.

themes: the enemy's arrogance as God's instrument

schools.summary: Gill: the young men come out of the city and march toward the Syrian camp, the host following.
K&D: the governors' servants lead and the rest of the army follows — the formation the prophet had appointed.
The Syrians, taken unready, are panic-struck and flee; K&D note Benhadad escapes on horseback, and Ellicott compares Saul's and Gideon's routs, doubting the event need be miraculous.
Henry: the despised Israelites are more than conquerors; God takes away the spirit of princes and makes himself terrible to kings.
Clarke: the Syrians were doubtless panic-struck.

debates.question: Was the rout miraculous or a natural collapse?

sides.position: a natural panic and dispersion, not necessarily miraculous
a manifest act of God taking away the spirit of princes

schools.summary: K&D: Ahab goes out and smites the horses and chariots with great slaughter; Gill adds, from Josephus, a reserve army within the walls.
Henry: Ahab improves the advantage; God often makes one wicked man a scourge to another.

themes: one wicked man a scourge to another

schools.summary: 'At the return of the year' is the next spring, 'when kings go out to battle'; the prophet warns Ahab to strengthen himself for a renewed Syrian assault.
Henry: God decreed the end, but Ahab must use the means; the church's enemies are restless, so grace received is no ground for slackness.
Gill: the warning comes from the same prophet who gave the promise.

The 'gods of the hills' notion springs from Israel's mountain sanctuaries and hill warfare; but shrewd policy lurks under the superstition — the plain suits Syrian chariots, and replacing the kings centralizes power.
Clarke: Israel used the heights against the cavalry; the heathen believed each district had a tutelary deity powerless outside its sphere.
Henry: the Syrians speak ignorantly of Jehovah — as many, local, and confined to hills — vain in their imaginations of God.
K&D locate Aphek in the plain of Jezreel near Endor, chosen because Benhadad meant to fight on the plain; the command is reorganized under professional captains.
Clarke: the unskilled kings are replaced by experienced captains, and the fight is to be on the plains for sure victory.
Henry: commanders by merit for commanders by birth; Syria was rich enough to rebuild horse for horse and chariot for chariot.
Gill: Benhadad is bidden raise an army of equal number, which his counsellors assume he can do.
Henry: that Syria could replace its host 'horse for horse, chariot for chariot' shows how rich and populous the kingdom was.
'Aphek' means 'fortress' and names several sites; Ellicott offers Esdraelon or a town east of Galilee, K&D the plain of Jezreel near Endor — all on the plain Benhadad deliberately chose.
Clarke places this Aphek far north in Lebanon on the river Adonis, between Heliopolis and Biblos.
Gill marks the season — the return of the year, in the spring.

sides.position: a town on the plain (Esdraelon / Jezreel near Endor / east of Galilee)
far north in Lebanon on the river Adonis

schools.summary: Israel, though victualled and supplied, encamps 'like two little flocks of goats' against Syrians who fill the land; Ellicott infers earlier defeats had broken Israel's strength.
Henry: small, meanly equipped, and contemptible in appearance, Israel is the feeblest of flocks set against a country full of Syrian numbers and baggage.

The victory answers the Syrians' blasphemy that Jehovah is a mere mountain-god; it vindicates God's majesty before the heathen, like the later rebuke of Sennacherib. Ellicott thinks this a different prophet.
Clarke: God resents and punishes the blasphemy, showing his power is everywhere and a great host is nothing against him.
Henry: not for Ahab's sake (who neither prayed nor praised) but because the Syrians blasphemed the divine omnipotence.
On the seventh day the battle is joined and 100,000 Syrian footmen fall in one day; Clarke notes the enormous number has no textual variant.
Gill: Israel pitched on a hill, and the Syrians waited for them to move before attacking.
Theodoret (quoted by K&D): the disaster fell out that both nations might learn the plague was heaven-sent.

themes: a hundred thousand in one day

schools.summary: The wall falls on 27,000 survivors at Aphek; K&D read a special divine interposition (against Thenius's undermining theory), Ellicott leaves earthquake or storming open and flags the large numbers.
Henry: probably an earthquake; the men found their bane where they sought shelter, and Benhadad's great confidence ends in cowardly hiding.
Clarke suspects God's more immediate hand, and previews his theory that 'wall' may mean a burning wind.

debates.question: What caused the wall to fall on the 27,000?

sides.position: a special, miraculous interposition of God (earthquake or direct act)
a natural cause — undermining/storming, or a blasting wind

schools.summary: The sackcloth and ropes are the language of abject unconditional surrender, likened by both to the burghers of Calais before Edward III.
Henry: Israel's kings are merciful because their God is; the scene pictures the gospel encouragement of sinners to venture on divine mercy.
Gill: trapped by the fallen wall, the Syrians throw themselves on Ahab's clemency as their only hope.
K&D: flattered that his powerful foe must beg his favour, Ahab spares him without recalling how Saul had been blamed for a like act; Clarke marks the reversal of fortune.
Henry: 'he is my brother' is self-flattery — Ahab values his royalty above his religion and is proud to be courted by the man he had feared.
Gill: the Syrians come as captives, ropes on their heads, ready to be hanged if Ahab pleased.
The envoys watch 'as for augury' and catch up Ahab's word 'brother' as a binding pledge of amity, which he seals by taking Benhadad into his chariot.
Clarke: they watched for a favourable omen, and 'brother' gave them much encouragement.
Gill: they observed Ahab as soothsayers watch for a lucky sign, the word bearing the sense of divining.

themes: a pledge not to be recalled

schools.summary: The restored cities and the 'streets' (trading quarters/squares) in Damascus imply overlordship; but the covenant was made hastily, without the prophetic sanction due after a God-given victory.
Henry: Ahab made the covenant without consulting prophets or elders, let Benhadad set his own terms, and never reproved his blasphemy — success ill bestowed.
Gill (with Osiander): the concessions may be Ahab's own words, the victor generously dictating the enemy's reparations.

debates.question: Whose words open the verse's offer of cities and streets?

sides.position: Benhadad's, promising restitution to keep Ahab favourable
Ahab's own, in superabundant kindness dictating the terms

schools.summary: A disciple of the prophets, in the word of the LORD, prepares a symbolic action (like Nathan's) to make Ahab judge himself; 'sons of the prophets' means disciples in schools revived to secure the victory over Baal.
Clarke: the command to smite was the Lord's, so refusal forfeited the man's life.
Gill relays the tradition that the prophet was Micaiah, whom Ahab later recognizes.

themes: the word of the LORD not trifled with

schools.summary: The lion that slays the disobedient disciple shows how severely God punishes opposition to his command, paralleling the prophet of 1 Kings 13:24.
Clarke: a hard measure but with ample reason — the man knew God's ways and the smiting had no evil in it when commanded.
Henry: if a good prophet is slain for sparing when God said smite, far worse is the king who spared his God-doomed enemy; mercy against God's judgment is rebellion.
Gill: the second man, a rustic and no prophet, does not hesitate to smite him at his word.
K&D: 'smiting and wounding' — the blow draws blood, that the prophet may show Ahab symbolically what to expect from the released Benhadad.
The disguise is best read as a bandage over the eyes/face (matching the LXX and Chaldee), not ashes, to look like a wounded soldier; the wound itself has symbolic significance.
Clarke: following Houbigant, read 'fillet/bandage' not 'dust,' the words differing by one letter's corner, and the old versions confirm it.
Gill: the prophet waits by the road from Aphek to Samaria.

debates.question: 'Ashes upon his face' or a bandage?

sides.position: a head-band/bandage over the eyes (LXX telamon, Chaldee cloth)
literally ashes/dust, as the received text reads

schools.summary: The fictitious tale of a lost prisoner (life for life, or a talent of silver) is a Nathan-like parable; but unlike David, Ahab responds with sullen displeasure, as over Naboth's vineyard.
Clarke: the drift is obvious, yet Ahab is led to pass sentence on himself.
Gill: the disguised man cries aloud, and the king stops to hear him.
K&D: 'thus thy sentence, thou hast decided' — Ahab pronounces his own judgment and must bear the stated penalty.
Gill: the plea of being 'busy here and there' is the excuse of distracted men who lose what was entrusted; Ahab rules against it firmly.
Gill: the prophet takes the cloth from his eyes and unmasks himself, and Ahab knows him for one of the prophets.
Clarke: the covering was thin cloth he could see through, a sufficient disguise until he removed it.

Benhadad was 'the man of my ban,' devoted to death by God (Leviticus 27:29); Ahab's release of him — like Saul's sparing of Agag — usurps God's judgment, and the doom recoils, fulfilled at Ramoth-gilead.
Henry: 'Thou art the man'; life for life and people for people, fulfilled later in Syria's ravaging of Israel — foolish pity spoils the city.
Clarke notes the fulfilment of 'thy life for his life' when Ahab fell at Ramoth-gilead.

debates.question: Was Ahab's sparing of Benhadad compassion or sin?

sides.position: a real sin — misplaced lenity usurping God's judgment, treason to righteousness
partly compassion and weakness, yet still without right to stand against God's sentence

schools.summary: Henry: Ahab goes home not penitent but enraged at the prophet and exasperated at God — heaviness is not humility; be wise, O ye kings.
Clarke: had he been displeased with himself and humbled his soul, the judgment might have been averted; he appends notes on a possible 'burning wind' and the burghers of Calais.
Gill: Ahab is displeased both with the prophet for his word and with himself for releasing Benhadad.
